Threat.value: Having strong negative feelings such as fear, horror, anger, guilt, or shame
Having strong physical reactions when something reminded you of the stressful experience
Being superalert or watchful or on guard

Memory.value: When memories come to mind of what happened, I feel my body is in the same position as when the frightening event occurred.
I remember the frightening event as a few moments, and each moment is a picture in my mind.
My memories of the frightening event are very clear and detailed.

Negative Appraisals.value: I can't trust that I will do the right thing
I feel like an object, not like a person

vignette:
She is a 60-year-old European woman in an ongoing relationship who presented with marked post-traumatic stress symptoms following a military trauma. Her PCL-5 score of 52 reflected a substantial level of distress and functional impact. In daily life she is easily unsettled by situations that remind her of being out of control, by comments about family, and by crowded or overstimulating environments. These moments quickly shift her into a state of alarm, with intense fear, anger, guilt, or shame, alongside strong physical reactions and a persistent sense of being on guard.

When reminders arise, her memories return in a fragmented yet forceful way. She describes them as a series of snapshot-like scenes, vivid and detailed, and at times accompanied by a bodily re-experiencing in which she feels her body is again in the same position as during the frightening event. These memory intrusions are closely tied to her heightened threat state, leaving her feeling unsafe and exposed long after the original event.

She also holds deeply negative views of herself and her future. She speaks in hopeless terms, saying she has no future, and struggles with self-doubt, feeling she cannot trust herself to do the right thing. At other times she describes feeling like an object rather than a person, reflecting a profound sense of dehumanization. These appraisals sit alongside her ongoing sense of threat and appear to colour her experience of the world as unsafe and her place in it as diminished.

In an attempt to manage these experiences, she avoids crowded places, carries a weapon, and uses heavy make-up. Her avoidance has narrowed her day-to-day life and reduced her confidence in social and public settings, while her relationship is strained by her guardedness and emotional distance. Overall, she appears caught in a cycle in which reminders of the trauma provoke vivid recollection and heightened threat, and the persistent sense of danger and loss of self continues to maintain her distress.
